fidelity_problems: Debugging example math is wrong: given its priors and likelihoods, P(code bug | mobile-only) is about 0.63, not ~0.55.
The A/B test example treats p=0.08 as if it can be casually converted into P(result | works) and P(result | doesn't work); p-values are not likelihood ratios or posterior probabilities.
The likelihood-ratio table gives arbitrary 'typical' numeric ranges, encouraging fake precision.
Sequential updating omits the conditional-independence requirement, so agents may double-count correlated evidence.
'Beliefs are probabilities' is too broad; Bayesian probabilities require clearly specified hypotheses/propositions and a likelihood model.
mislead_scenario: An agent sees an early A/B test with p=0.08, invents a 4x likelihood ratio like the example, updates a 30% prior to 63%, and recommends shipping—ignoring effect-size uncertainty, optional stopping, multiple comparisons, and weak evidence.
missing_boundaries: Do not force numeric posteriors when priors or likelihoods are guesses; use ranges or qualitative uncertainty instead.
Do not use without mutually exclusive/exhaustive hypotheses or allowance for 'unknown cause'.
Do not update repeatedly on correlated or selected evidence unless dependence is modeled.
Do not substitute this for causal inference, experiment design, power analysis, or decision analysis with utilities/costs.
Do not use for high-stakes medical/legal/safety conclusions without validated domain statistics and expert review.
weakest_part: The Step 2 A/B test likelihood-ratio example: it makes ad hoc numbers look mathematically authoritative.
highest_leverage_fix: Add a guardrail requiring sourced priors, explicit hypotheses, a justified likelihood model, independence/selection checks, and sensitivity analysis before producing any precise posterior.
